comment_text.sn44.1:1.1: The “undeclared points” are a stock series of propositions typically raised by non-Buddhists. It seems they formed a kind of scheme for evaluating and positioning different philosophies, which were assumed to answer them definitively. They are discussed many times in the Suttas, eg. SN 16.12, SN 24.15–18, SN 33.1–55.
comment_text.sn44.1:1.2: Khemā was extolled as the foremost nun in great wisdom (AN 1.236); her verses are at Thig 6.3; see SN 17.24:1.7. | Toraṇavatthu (“the site of the arch”) appears only here.
comment_text.sn44.1:1.5: Pasenadi uses the default masculine forms, the significance of which becomes apparent below.
comment_text.sn44.1:3.5: Pasenadi was used to having philosophical conversations with his wife, Queen Mallikā (SN 3.8:1.2).
comment_text.sn44.1:4.1: The king bows to the nun, just as he would to the Buddha.
comment_text.sn44.1:4.2: That is, an awakened one, whether the Buddha or anyone else, exists after death, for example in heaven or an eternal state of Nirvana. | Here “realized one” (tathāgata) pertains to any arahant, as at SN 54.12. The Pali commentary says that a “living being” (satta) is meant. However, tathāgata and satta are exact verbal parallels: tathā (“real”) + gata (“come to the state of”) and sa (“real”) + tta (“state of”). Thus it seems likely that this was originally intended as a mere verbal gloss to explain the compound. That it does not mean just any sentient being is established at SN 22.86:1.5.
comment_text.sn44.1:4.5: A sage ceases to exist at the time of death. This assumes there is an enduring “self” that is done away with.
comment_text.sn44.1:4.8: This kind of “both A and not A” statements are not meant to be paradoxical or mystical. Rather, they represent some state that combines elements of both. For example, their body does not exist but their mind does.
comment_text.sn44.1:4.11: Likewise, “neither A nor not A” is not meant to be a paradox, but to indicate something that cannot be characterized by the original term. For example, a sage persists after death in a subtle state that is not what we normally consider existence.
comment_text.sn44.1:6.3: Accountant (gaṇaka), finger-tallier (muddika), and reckoner (saṅkhāyako) are terms for the job that used to be called “computer”.
comment_text.sn44.1:13.2: Text has virodhayissati (“contradict”), wheras AN 11.7:10.2 has variant viggayhissati (“conflict”).

root_text.sn44.1:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn44.1:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena khemā bhikkhunī kosalesu cārikaṁ caramānā antarā ca sāvatthiṁ antarā ca sāketaṁ toraṇavatthusmiṁ vāsaṁ upagatā hoti.
root_text.sn44.1:1.3: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo sāketā sāvatthiṁ gacchanto, antarā ca sāketaṁ antarā ca sāvatthiṁ toraṇavatthusmiṁ ekarattivāsaṁ upagacchi.
root_text.sn44.1:1.4: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo aññataraṁ purisaṁ āmantesi:
root_text.sn44.1:1.5: “ehi tvaṁ, ambho purisa, toraṇavatthusmiṁ tathārūpaṁ samaṇaṁ vā brāhmaṇaṁ vā jāna yamahaṁ ajja payirupāseyyan”ti.
root_text.sn44.1:2.1: “Evaṁ, devā”ti kho so puriso rañño pasenadissa kosalassa paṭissutvā kevalakappaṁ toraṇavatthuṁ āhiṇḍanto nāddasa tathārūpaṁ samaṇaṁ vā brāhmaṇaṁ vā yaṁ rājā pasenadi kosalo payirupāseyya.
root_text.sn44.1:2.2: Addasā kho so puriso khemaṁ bhikkhuniṁ toraṇavatthusmiṁ vāsaṁ upagataṁ.
root_text.sn44.1:2.3: Disvāna yena rājā pasenadi kosalo tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā rājānaṁ pasenadiṁ kosalaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn44.1:3.1: “Natthi kho, deva, toraṇavatthusmiṁ tathārūpo samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā yaṁ devo payirupāseyya.
root_text.sn44.1:3.2: Atthi ca kho, deva, khemā nāma bhikkhunī, tassa bhagavato sāvikā arahato sammāsambuddhassa.
root_text.sn44.1:3.3: Tassā kho pana ayyāya evaṁ kalyāṇo kittisaddo abbhuggato:
root_text.sn44.1:4.1: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo yena khemā bhikkhunī tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā khemaṁ bhikkhuniṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho rājā pasenadi kosalo khemaṁ bhikkhuniṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn44.1:4.2: “kiṁ nu kho, ayye, hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:4.5: “Kiṁ panayye, na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:4.8: “Kiṁ nu kho, ayye, hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:4.10: ‘hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’”ti.
root_text.sn44.1:4.11: “Kiṁ panayye, neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā”ti.
root_text.sn44.1:4.13: ‘neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’”ti.
root_text.sn44.1:5.1: “‘Kiṁ nu kho, ayye, hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti, iti puṭṭhā samānā:
root_text.sn44.1:5.4: ‘Kiṁ panayye, na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti iti puṭṭhā samānā:
root_text.sn44.1:5.6: na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti vadesi.
root_text.sn44.1:5.7: ‘Kiṁ nu kho, ayye, hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti iti puṭṭhā samānā:
root_text.sn44.1:5.9: hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti vadesi.
root_text.sn44.1:5.10: ‘Kiṁ panayye, neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti iti puṭṭhā samānā:
root_text.sn44.1:5.12: neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti vadesi.
root_text.sn44.1:5.13: Ko nu kho, ayye, hetu, ko paccayo yenetaṁ abyākataṁ bhagavatā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:6.1: “Tena hi, mahārāja, taññevettha paṭipucchissāmi. Yathā te khameyya tathā naṁ byākareyyāsi.
root_text.sn44.1:6.3: atthi te koci gaṇako vā muddiko vā saṅkhāyako vā yo pahoti gaṅgāya vālukaṁ gaṇetuṁ—
root_text.sn44.1:6.4: ettakā vālukā iti vā, ettakāni vālukasatāni iti vā, ettakāni vālukasahassāni iti vā, ettakāni vālukasatasahassāni iti vā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:6.6: “Atthi pana te koci gaṇako vā muddiko vā saṅkhāyako vā yo pahoti mahāsamudde udakaṁ gaṇetuṁ—
root_text.sn44.1:6.7: ettakāni udakāḷhakāni iti vā, ettakāni udakāḷhakasatāni iti vā, ettakāni udakāḷhakasahassāni iti vā, ettakāni udakāḷhakasatasahassāni iti vā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:6.11: “Evameva kho, mahārāja, yena rūpena tathāgataṁ paññāpayamāno paññāpeyya taṁ rūpaṁ tathāgatassa pahīnaṁ ucchinnamūlaṁ tālāvatthukataṁ anabhāvaṅkataṁ āyatiṁ anuppādadhammaṁ.
root_text.sn44.1:6.12: Rūpasaṅkhāyavimutto kho, mahārāja, tathāgato gambhīro appameyyo duppariyogāho—
root_text.sn44.1:6.14: ‘Hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti.
root_text.sn44.1:7.1: Yāya vedanāya tathāgataṁ paññāpayamāno paññāpeyya, sā vedanā tathāgatassa pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn44.1:7.2: Vedanāsaṅkhāyavimutto, mahārāja, tathāgato gambhīro appameyyo duppariyogāho—
root_text.sn44.1:7.4: ‘Hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti.
root_text.sn44.1:8.2: yehi saṅkhārehi tathāgataṁ paññāpayamāno paññāpeyya, te saṅkhārā tathāgatassa pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn44.1:8.3: Saṅkhārasaṅkhāyavimutto kho, mahārāja, tathāgato gambhīro appameyyo duppariyogāho—
root_text.sn44.1:8.5: ‘Hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti.
root_text.sn44.1:9.1: Yena viññāṇena tathāgataṁ paññāpayamāno paññāpeyya taṁ viññāṇaṁ tathāgatassa pahīnaṁ ucchinnamūlaṁ tālāvatthukataṁ anabhāvaṅkataṁ āyatiṁ anuppādadhammaṁ.
root_text.sn44.1:9.2: Viññāṇasaṅkhāyavimutto kho, mahārāja, tathāgato gambhīro appameyyo duppariyogāho—
root_text.sn44.1:9.4: ‘Hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upetī”ti.
root_text.sn44.1:9.5: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo khemāya bhikkhuniyā bhāsitaṁ abhinanditvā anumoditvā uṭṭhāyāsanā khemaṁ bhikkhuniṁ abhivādetvā padakkhiṇaṁ katvā pakkāmi.
root_text.sn44.1:10.1: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo aparena samayena yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho rājā pasenadi kosalo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn44.1:10.2: “kiṁ nu kho, bhante, hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:10.5: “Kiṁ pana, bhante, na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:10.8: “Kiṁ nu kho, bhante, hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:10.10: ‘hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’”ti.
root_text.sn44.1:10.11: “Kiṁ pana, bhante, neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:10.13: ‘neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’”ti.
root_text.sn44.1:10.14: “‘Kiṁ nu kho, bhante, hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti iti puṭṭho samāno:
root_text.sn44.1:10.15: ‘abyākataṁ kho etaṁ, mahārāja, mayā—hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti vadesi …pe….
root_text.sn44.1:10.16: “‘Kiṁ pana, bhante, neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti iti puṭṭho samāno:
root_text.sn44.1:10.18: neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti vadesi.
root_text.sn44.1:10.19: Ko nu kho, bhante, hetu, ko paccayo, yenetaṁ abyākataṁ bhagavatā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:11.1: “Tena hi, mahārāja, taññevettha paṭipucchissāmi. Yathā te khameyya tathā naṁ byākareyyāsi.
root_text.sn44.1:11.2: Taṁ kiṁ maññasi, mahārāja, atthi te koci gaṇako vā muddiko vā saṅkhāyako vā yo pahoti gaṅgāya vālukaṁ gaṇetuṁ—
root_text.sn44.1:11.3: ettakā vālukā iti vā …pe… ettakāni vālukasatasahassāni iti vā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:11.5: “Atthi pana te koci gaṇako vā muddiko vā saṅkhāyako vā yo pahoti mahāsamudde udakaṁ gaṇetuṁ—
root_text.sn44.1:11.6: ettakāni udakāḷhakāni iti vā …pe… ettakāni udakāḷhakasatasahassāni iti vā”ti?
root_text.sn44.1:11.10: Evameva kho, mahārāja, yena rūpena tathāgataṁ paññāpayamāno paññāpeyya, taṁ rūpaṁ tathāgatassa pahīnaṁ ucchinnamūlaṁ tālāvatthukataṁ anabhāvaṅkataṁ āyatiṁ anuppādadhammaṁ.
root_text.sn44.1:11.11: Rūpasaṅkhāyavimutto kho, mahārāja, tathāgato gambhīro appameyyo duppariyogāho—
root_text.sn44.1:11.14: ‘neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti.
root_text.sn44.1:12.1: Yena viññāṇena tathāgataṁ paññāpayamāno paññāpeyya, taṁ viññāṇaṁ tathāgatassa pahīnaṁ ucchinnamūlaṁ tālāvatthukataṁ anabhāvaṅkataṁ āyatiṁ anuppādadhammaṁ.
root_text.sn44.1:12.2: Viññāṇasaṅkhāyavimutto kho, mahārāja, tathāgato gambhīro appameyyo duppariyogāho—
root_text.sn44.1:12.4: ‘Hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upeti, ‘neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’tipi na upetī”ti.
root_text.sn44.1:13.2: Yatra hi nāma satthu ceva sāvikāya ca atthena attho byañjanena byañjanaṁ saṁsandissati, samessati, na virodhayissati yadidaṁ aggapadasmiṁ.
root_text.sn44.1:13.3: Ekamidāhaṁ, bhante, samayaṁ khemaṁ bhikkhuniṁ upasaṅkamitvā etamatthaṁ apucchiṁ.
root_text.sn44.1:13.4: Sāpi me ayyā etehi padehi etehi byañjanehi etamatthaṁ byākāsi, seyyathāpi bhagavā.
root_text.sn44.1:13.6: Yatra hi nāma satthu ceva sāvikāya ca atthena attho byañjanena byañjanaṁ saṁsandissati, samessati, na virodhayissati yadidaṁ aggapadasmiṁ.
root_text.sn44.1:13.10: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo bhagavato bhāsitaṁ abhinanditvā anumoditvā uṭṭhāyāsanā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā padakkhiṇaṁ katvā pakkāmīti.

translation_text.sn44.1:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn44.1:1.2: Now at that time the nun Khemā was wandering in the land of the Kosalans between Sāvatthī and Sāketa when she took up residence in Toraṇavatthu.
translation_text.sn44.1:1.3: Then King Pasenadi was traveling from Sāketa to Sāvatthī, and he too stayed in Toraṇavatthu for a single night.
translation_text.sn44.1:1.4: Then King Pasenadi addressed a man,
translation_text.sn44.1:1.5: “Please, my friend, check if there’s a suitable (male) ascetic or brahmin in Toraṇavatthu to whom I can pay homage.”
translation_text.sn44.1:2.1: “Yes, Your Majesty,” replied that man. He searched all over Toraṇavatthu, but he couldn’t see a suitable ascetic or brahmin for the king to pay homage to.
translation_text.sn44.1:2.2: But he saw that the nun Khemā was staying there,
translation_text.sn44.1:2.3: so he went to the king and said to him,
translation_text.sn44.1:3.1: “Your Majesty, there’s no (male) ascetic or brahmin in Toraṇavatthu for the king to pay homage to.
translation_text.sn44.1:3.2: But there is the nun Khemā, who’s a disciple of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.
translation_text.sn44.1:3.3: She has a good reputation as being
translation_text.sn44.1:3.4: astute, competent, clever, learned, a brilliant speaker, and eloquent.
translation_text.sn44.1:3.5: Your Majesty may pay homage to her.”
translation_text.sn44.1:4.1: Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the nun Khemā, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to her:
translation_text.sn44.1:4.2: “Ma’am, does a realized one still exist after death?”
translation_text.sn44.1:4.3: “Great king, this has not been declared by the Buddha.”
translation_text.sn44.1:4.5: “Well then, does a realized one no longer exist after death?”
translation_text.sn44.1:4.6: “This too has not been declared by the Buddha.”
translation_text.sn44.1:4.8: “Well then, does a realized one both exist and not exist after death?”
translation_text.sn44.1:4.9: “This has not been declared by the Buddha.”
translation_text.sn44.1:4.11: “Well then, does a realized one neither exist nor not exist after death?”
translation_text.sn44.1:4.12: “This too has not been declared by the Buddha.”
translation_text.sn44.1:5.1: “Ma’am, when asked these questions, you say
translation_text.sn44.1:5.2: that this has not been declared by the Buddha.
translation_text.sn44.1:5.13: What’s the cause, what’s the reason why this has not been declared by the Buddha?”
translation_text.sn44.1:6.1: “Well then, great king, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like.
translation_text.sn44.1:6.2: What do you think, great king?
translation_text.sn44.1:6.3: Do you have any accountant or finger-tallier or reckoner who can count the grains of sand in the Ganges, that is,
translation_text.sn44.1:6.4: how many grains of sand there are, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of grains of sand?”
translation_text.sn44.1:6.6: “Do you have any accountant or finger-tallier or reckoner who can count the water in the ocean, that is,
translation_text.sn44.1:6.7: how many gallons of water there are, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of gallons of water?”
translation_text.sn44.1:6.10: Because the ocean is deep, immeasurable, and hard to fathom.”
translation_text.sn44.1:6.11: “In the same way, great king, any form by which a realized one might be described has been cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn44.1:6.12: A realized one is freed from reckoning in terms of form. They’re deep, immeasurable, and hard to fathom,
translation_text.sn44.1:6.14: To say that after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists: none of these apply.
translation_text.sn44.1:9.1: consciousness by which a realized one might be described has been cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn44.1:9.2: A realized one is freed from reckoning in terms of consciousness. They’re deep, immeasurable, and hard to fathom,
translation_text.sn44.1:9.4: To say that after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists: none of these apply.”
translation_text.sn44.1:9.5: Then King Pasenadi approved and agreed with what the nun Khemā said. Then he got up from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled her, keeping her on his right, before leaving.
translation_text.sn44.1:10.1: Then on a later occasion King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. He asked the Buddha exactly the same questions he had asked the nun Khemā, and received the same answers.
translation_text.sn44.1:13.1: He said, “It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing!
translation_text.sn44.1:13.2: How the meaning and the phrasing of the teacher and the disciple fit together and agree without contradiction when it comes to the chief matter!
translation_text.sn44.1:13.3: This one time I went to the nun Khemā and asked her about this matter.
translation_text.sn44.1:13.4: And she explained it to me with these words and phrases, just like the Buddha.
translation_text.sn44.1:13.6: How the meaning and the phrasing of the teacher and the disciple fit together and agree without contradiction when it comes to the chief matter!
translation_text.sn44.1:13.7: Well, now, sir, I must go.
translation_text.sn44.1:13.8: I have many duties, and much to do.”
translation_text.sn44.1:13.9: “Please, great king, go at your convenience.”
translation_text.sn44.1:13.10: Then King Pasenadi approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. Then he got up from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled him, keeping him on his right, before leaving.

variant_text.sn44.1:13.2: satthu ceva → satthuno ceva (bj); satthuno (pts1ed) | virodhayissati → vihāyissati (bj, sya-all, km); vigāyissati (mr)
